oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Wildlife Rehabilitation
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Darden, Jennifer
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 3 (April 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.We are excited to add a new feature to Watchbird to explain the role of wildlife rehabilitators and to get an ‘up close and personal’ look at some of the incredible raptors they work with. While raptors are birds of prey, not all birds of prey are raptors. There are three anatomical features that determine if a bird is a raptor: 1) a sharp beak with a hooked upper beak for tearing meat from prey rather than killing; 2) superb eyesight with eyes facing forward unlike other birds ; and 3) sharp claws or talons used for killing and gripping prey as well as for protection.A story about a different raptor will be featured in the next several issues. Our first article about Redtailed hawks begins on page 20. But first please take a minute to read about wildlife rehabilitation.Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 3 (2022): Watchbird April 2022; 19
